prompt:
Every service on the platform has grown its own half-broken middleware helpers, so we're blessing one tiny kit. Module go-mwchain, package mw, stdlib only. mw_test.go is the contract — read it, don't edit it.

Surface:
- type Middleware func(http.Handler) http.Handler
- Chain(mws ...Middleware) Middleware — Chain(a,b,c) runs a outermost (a sees the request first and the response last). Chain() is the identity, and a Chain must itself be usable as a Middleware so teams can nest them.
- RequestID — usable directly as a Middleware. Takes the id from an incoming X-Request-ID header, otherwise generates one (unique per request); puts it on the response header AND into the request context. RequestIDFrom(ctx) string reads it back, empty string when the middleware never ran. Use an unexported context key type, obviously.
- RequireHeader(name, want string) Middleware — gate for internal endpoints: header must equal want or the request dies right there with a 401 and the downstream handler must never run. Middleware outside the gate still runs.
- Recover — panics downstream become a plain 500 (don't echo the panic value to the client, that's how we leaked a connection string last year). http.ErrAbortHandler is special: re-panic it, net/http depends on that. After recovering one request the handler must keep working for the next.
- CaptureStatus(onDone func(status int, bytes int64)) Middleware — wraps the ResponseWriter and reports, once per request after the downstream returns, the status actually sent and total body bytes written. Rules the ops dashboards depend on: handler never calls WriteHeader -> 200; handler writes nothing at all -> 200/0; only the FIRST WriteHeader counts; a Write before any WriteHeader locks the status at 200; byte count sums every Write. And the wrapper must not lose http.Flusher — the SSE endpoints type-assert for it.

go test -race -timeout 30s ./... green is done. One file mw.go is fine, split it if you like.
